turns.content: I've read the docs on the nightly Allocator trait but I still don't get how it actually changes things in a real system. It sounds like just another wrapper for Vec, right? Why should I care about per-collection allocators when standard heap allocation is fine?

You're right that it looks like a simple extension to Vec<T, A>, but the real value comes from decoupling memory management from program logic. When you pass an arena allocator like Bumpalo to collections such as HashMap or LinkedList, those data structures can be moved into the heap without triggering individual allocations for each element. This means your program only touches the allocator once instead of millions of times during runtime.
So I just hand a single pointer to Vec and everything shares it? That sounds too simple. What if I need different allocators for different collections in one program?

Exactly, that's the power of the trait system here. You can define multiple allocator types like MyArena or CustomHeap and then specify which allocator each collection uses via its generic parameter Vec<T, A>. This lets you mix zero-copy arena allocations with full heap allocations in the same binary without any runtime overhead.
But what happens when I try to move a Vec between threads? Does the allocator get copied or does it cause deadlocks?

The allocator itself is just an object you pass by value, but the actual memory backing the collection lives in the heap. When you move a Vec<T, A> across threads, only the pointer moves; no allocation happens inside the move operation because the data already exists on the heap. The key insight is that your program never touches the allocator again after initialization.
This sounds great but I'm worried about safety. What if I accidentally use the wrong allocator for a collection and get panics?

The compiler enforces this at type-check time rather than runtime, so you can't create a mismatched scenario unless you deliberately write code that violates trait bounds. The trait system ensures compatibility before your program even runs. You might see a compile error if someone tries to use an incompatible allocator with Vec<T>, but the runtime will never panic due to this.
I understand the concept now, but how do I actually implement this in my project? Do I need a custom crate or something?

You don't need a new crate. The nightly Allocator API is built into Rust's standard library through traits and generics. You just define your own allocator type that implements the trait, then pass it to collections like Vec or HashMap when building them.
Can you show me a concrete example where I'm using Bumpalo with some collections?

Sure, imagine having an arena allocator called MyArena that wraps Bumpalo. Then you could create collections like Vec<MyData, MyArena> or HashMap<Key, Value, MyArena>. The trick is that these allocations happen entirely within the arena's bounds.
But what if I need to free memory manually? Does Bumpalo handle that automatically?

Yes, Bumpalo handles deallocation automatically when you drop the arena. The allocator trait doesn't require manual cleanup; it's designed so that all allocations are tied to the lifetime of the allocator object itself.
Okay I think I get it but one last thing - what if I want to mix different allocators in a single program?

That's actually the main use case. You can define multiple allocator types and assign them to different collections based on their needs. For example, using Bumpalo for small structures but full heap allocation for large ones.

I just implemented a custom allocator using the new nightly trait and I'm passing it to my Vec<T, A> everywhere. It works but feels like a lot of boilerplate for every collection. Is there a better way to manage this?

The reason you're seeing that boilerplate is because the Allocator trait requires explicit ownership at the type level, so every Vec<T, A> or HashMap<K, V, A> needs its own instance of your allocator. This design forces clarity on where memory comes from, but it does mean you can't just pass a single global pointer to all collections without wrapping them. The better approach for most cases is using an arena like Bumpalo which owns the entire pool and delegates allocation requests internally.
So I should stop creating my own allocator instances and just use Bumpalo instead? But what if I need to allocate something outside of that main arena?

You can definitely mix them by wrapping your custom allocator in a trait object or using the Allocator trait with a fallback. If you want to avoid per-collection allocations entirely, Bumpalo's integration lets you pass an ArenaAlloc into Vec and collections, so they all share one big block. This is especially useful when dealing with large datasets where fragmentation becomes a problem.
That makes sense for shared memory but what about thread safety? Can I safely use the same allocator across multiple threads?

The Allocator trait itself doesn't guarantee thread safety, so you need to ensure your specific implementation handles concurrent access correctly. Bumpalo's ArenaAlloc is designed with this in mind and uses internal locking or separate pools per thread depending on your configuration. For most applications, using the nightly Allocator API with a properly synchronized allocator pattern avoids race conditions.

I'm trying to implement a custom allocator using the new nightly features, but I can't get my Vec<T> to use it correctly. My code looks like this: let data = std::vec::Vec::<T, MyAllocator>::new(); and then I push items into it expecting them to come from my arena, but they aren't being allocated from my custom allocator.

It sounds like you're mixing up the standard library Vec type with the new Allocator API. The standard `std::vec::Vec<T>` does not support generic allocators yet, so your code won't compile or behave as expected. You need to use the nightly feature's specific types instead.
Ah okay, I thought standard Vec would just work if I added a trait bound. So what exactly should I be using? Is it still `std::vec::Vec` or do I need to import something else?

You definitely shouldn't use `std::vec::Vec`. Instead, you must use the nightly feature's `allocators` crate and its `Vec<T, A>` type. This is a distinct type that wraps your allocator `A` and allows collections to operate with it.
Got it, so I should be using `allocators::vec::Vec<T, MyAllocator>`. But how do I actually pass my arena allocator into this Vec? Do I need a special constructor or is there an implicit conversion?
You don't get it with `std::vec::Vec` at all. You must use the nightly feature's specific types instead.
